title: The Web Frontend
subtitle: What users see and click in the browser
intro: The frontend is the actual TesTly website. It is the part you can see and touch: the pages, buttons, forms, and charts that patients, labs, and admins use every day.
bullets: One website serves three kinds of users: patients, lab staff, and admins.
Anyone can browse labs and prices without signing up.
It talks to the backend server over the internet to get and save data.
Built with Next.js and React, two popular tools for making modern websites.

title: Tech Stack & Why
intro: The main tools we used to build the website, and why we picked each one.

cards.name: Next.js (App Router)
cards.what: A framework (a ready-made toolkit) for building websites with pages, navigation, and fast loading.
cards.why: It handles page routing, speed, and a security guard at the edge for us, so we write less low-level code.

cards.name: React
cards.what: A library for building screens out of small reusable pieces called components, like Lego blocks.
cards.why: When data changes, React updates only the part of the screen that changed, which keeps everything fast and tidy.

cards.name: TypeScript
cards.what: JavaScript with type-checking: it makes you label what kind of data each thing is (number, text, etc.).
cards.why: It catches mistakes while we type, before users ever see a bug. Great safety net for a medical app.

cards.name: Tailwind CSS
cards.what: A way to style pages by adding small ready-made class names directly on the elements.
cards.why: Fast to build a consistent look (colors, spacing, rounded cards) without writing big separate style files.

cards.name: Radix UI
cards.what: A set of pre-built, accessible building blocks like dialogs (pop-up boxes) and labels.
cards.why: These tricky pieces are easy to get wrong (keyboard use, screen readers). Radix gets the hard parts right for us.

cards.name: Recharts
cards.what: A library that draws charts and graphs (lines, bars, areas) from data.
cards.why: We need clear health-trend line charts and admin dashboards; Recharts draws them with little effort.

cards.name: zod
cards.what: A tool that checks data really has the shape you expect, and complains loudly if not.
cards.why: We use it to validate settings (like the backend address) at build time, so a misconfigured app fails early instead of breaking for users.

cards.name: jose
cards.what: A small library that can read a login token (a JWT, a signed slip of identity info).
cards.why: The security guard inside Next.js uses it to peek at your role from the login token and decide which pages to show.

title: Other Key Libraries
intro: Smaller helpers that make the app smoother to build and nicer to use.

rows.name: lucide-react
rows.what: A collection of clean icons (little pictures) used all over the app.
rows.why: Consistent, professional-looking icons without drawing each one by hand.

rows.name: clsx + tailwind-merge
rows.what: Helpers that combine and clean up the styling class names on an element.
rows.why: They let us turn styles on and off safely, with no clashing or duplicate styles.

rows.name: Playwright
rows.what: A tool that opens the real website in a browser and clicks through it automatically.
rows.why: It tests the main journeys (search, login, booking) like a robot user, catching breakage before release.

title: Where the Frontend Fits
imageCaption: The browser website sits on the left and talks to the backend server, which holds the data.
bullets: The website never stores secret data itself; it asks the backend server for everything.
The same backend serves both the website and the phone app.
Charts, pages, and the AI chat are all just views of data the server sends back.

title: Role-Based Pages: Three Apps in One
intro: The same website shows different dashboards depending on who you are.
imageCaption: What a patient can do: browse, compare, book, see results, and view trends.

steps.title: Anyone (no login)
steps.detail: The public pages: the landing page, lab search, lab details, and test prices. You can browse and compare freely; no account needed.

steps.title: Patient dashboard
steps.detail: After logging in as a patient: your bookings, your results, your health-trend charts, your profile, and the AI assistant.

steps.title: Lab staff dashboard
steps.detail: For lab workers: a booking queue, their test catalogue, schedule, uploading results, and analytics about their lab.

steps.title: Admin dashboard
steps.detail: For platform admins: approving or rejecting labs, viewing platform-wide stats, and managing users.

title: How Login Stays Safe in the Browser
intro: Three simple ideas work together to keep your login secure.

steps.title: Step 1: A cookie the code can't read
steps.detail: When you log in, the server hands the browser a special login slip called an httpOnly cookie. 'httpOnly' means even the website's own JavaScript can't read it, so a hacker's script can't steal it.

steps.title: Step 2: A guard at the edge checks your role
steps.detail: Before showing a protected page, a small program (Edge middleware) peeks at your login slip and checks your role. A patient can't open the admin page; a non-active lab is sent to an explanation page.

steps.title: Step 3: A same-origin /api proxy keeps the cookie 'first-party'
steps.detail: The website and the server live on different web addresses. Browsers distrust cookies sent across different sites. So we route every request through the website's own address under /api, which quietly forwards it to the server. To the browser it all looks like one site, so the cookie is trusted and always sent.

steps.title: Step 4: The real check still happens on the server
steps.detail: The edge guard is just a fast first gate. The backend server re-checks every request properly, so even a faked login slip gets rejected. This is called defence in depth: two locks, not one.

title: How the AI Assistant Answers Live
intro: The health assistant types its answer to you word by word, and can show clickable cards.
imageCaption: Like booking, the assistant is a back-and-forth conversation between browser and server.

steps.title: Step 1: You send a question
steps.detail: For example, 'Where can I get a CBC test in Cairo?' The browser sends it to the server and opens a live connection.

steps.title: Step 2: The answer streams in (Server-Sent Events)
steps.detail: Instead of waiting for the whole reply, the server streams it back in small pieces over an open pipe called Server-Sent Events. The text appears gradually, like someone typing to you.

steps.title: Step 3: Clickable lab and test cards appear
steps.detail: The assistant can look up real labs and tests in the live catalogue and send them as cards. You can tap a card to jump straight to that lab or test page and book.

steps.title: Step 4: It marks the message as done
steps.detail: A final signal tells the browser the answer is complete, so the typing dots stop and the message is saved.

title: How Health-Trend Charts Are Drawn
intro: Patients can see their lab values over time, drawn as easy-to-read line charts.

steps.title: Step 1: Get the numbers
steps.detail: The browser asks the server for the patient's lab values over a chosen period (3, 6, or 12 months, or all time).

steps.title: Step 2: Draw a line per value
steps.detail: Recharts plots each measurement as a dot and joins the dots into a line, so you can see if a value is going up, down, or staying steady.

steps.title: Step 3: Shade the normal range
steps.detail: A coloured band (called a reference area) shows the healthy range. If your line is inside the band, that value looks normal; outside it stands out.

steps.title: Step 4: Colour the dots
steps.detail: Each dot is coloured: green if in range, red if abnormal, blue if unknown. A built-in disclaimer reminds users this is information, not medical advice.

title: Algorithm: Finding the Nearest Labs
intro: When you sort labs by 'Nearest', the website needs to know how far each lab is from you. It works this out right in your browser, with no server help.

steps.title: Ask for your location
steps.detail: The browser asks for permission to use your device's GPS or location. We keep watching it (watchPosition) so the position improves as a better fix arrives, and we politely retry if you grant permission late.

steps.title: Get each lab's coordinates
steps.detail: Every lab comes with its latitude and longitude (its spot on the map), sent from the server.

steps.title: Measure distance with the haversine formula
steps.detail: The haversine formula calculates the distance between two points on the round surface of the Earth, using their latitude and longitude. We run it for you-versus-each-lab to get a distance in kilometres.

steps.title: Sort by closeness
steps.detail: We sort the labs from nearest to farthest and show the distance on each card. All of this happens instantly in the browser.

why: It gives patients an honest 'nearest first' list without sending their location to the server, which is faster and more private.

title: Algorithm: Reading the Live AI Stream
intro: The AI answer arrives as a fast trickle of tiny text pieces. The browser must turn that messy stream into clean, meaningful events it can act on.

steps.title: Open the stream
steps.detail: The browser sends the question and starts reading the reply as it arrives, instead of waiting for the whole thing.

steps.title: Cut the stream into frames
steps.detail: The pieces arrive glued together. We split them at the blank lines that separate each message, so each chunk becomes one complete frame.

steps.title: Label each frame by type
steps.detail: Every frame is read and tagged as one of a few known kinds: 'meta' (which conversation), 'delta' (a bit of text), 'tool' (lab/test cards), 'done' (finished), or 'error'.

steps.title: Update the screen per type
steps.detail: Text frames get added to the growing message bubble; card frames become clickable cards; the 'done' frame stops the typing animation. Each type is handled safely with its expected shape.

why: Turning a raw, fast text stream into clearly-typed events makes the live chat reliable and lets it show both words and rich clickable cards.

title: Smart UX Details
intro: Small touches across every page that make the app feel polished and trustworthy.

cards.name: Toast messages
cards.what: Little pop-up notes in the corner that confirm an action ('Booking cancelled') or warn of an error.
cards.why: Users always get clear, instant feedback after every action, so nothing feels silent or broken.

cards.name: Loading skeletons
cards.what: Grey shimmering placeholders shown while real data is on its way.
cards.why: The page feels fast and alive instead of showing a blank screen while it waits.

cards.name: Smart, friendly errors
cards.what: The app knows the difference between 'not found', 'not allowed', and 'please log in', and reacts correctly to each.
cards.why: Users get a helpful message and the right next step instead of a confusing crash.

cards.name: Floating FAQ chatbot
cards.what: A simple help bubble on every page that answers common questions from a fixed list.
cards.why: Handles cheap, common questions instantly without using the costly AI, saving money and avoiding wrong answers.

title: Code: Nearest Labs Calculated in Your Browser
intro: When you sort by 'Nearest', the website runs the haversine formula right in your browser — no server round-trip needed. Your location stays on your device.
note: **`watchPosition`** (not `getCurrentPosition`) is used so the distance updates live as the GPS gets a better fix. The user's location is **never sent to the server** — it stays in the browser's memory. The same haversine formula is copied identically into the backend (TypeScript) and mobile app (Dart) so all three platforms show the same number for the same lab.

title: Code: Reading the Live AI Stream
intro: The AI answer arrives as a continuous trickle of text events. The frontend splits this raw stream into typed events and updates the screen for each one.
note: **Server-Sent Events (SSE)** is a one-way pipe: the browser opens one HTTP connection and the server keeps writing to it. No WebSocket setup needed. The `buffer` accumulates bytes until a complete `\n\n`-delimited frame arrives, then parses it as JSON. Each event type ('meta', 'delta', 'tool', 'done', 'error') triggers a different UI update — text frames grow the message bubble, tool frames insert clickable lab cards.

title: Q&A: Frontend Deep Questions
intro: Questions about how the web app is built and how specific features work.

pairs.q: Why is the login cookie 'httpOnly' and what does that protect against?
pairs.a: An `httpOnly` cookie cannot be read by JavaScript — only the browser can include it in HTTP requests. This blocks **XSS attacks**: if a malicious script somehow runs on the page (e.g. from an injected ad or a compromised package), it cannot steal the login cookie because `document.cookie` simply doesn't show it. The cookie travels to the server automatically on every request, invisible to all client-side code.

pairs.q: What is 'edge middleware' and why run the role check there?
pairs.a: Next.js Edge Middleware runs on Cloudflare/Vercel's network **before** the request reaches the actual server — physically closer to the user, typically under 1ms. It reads the JWT from the cookie (using the `jose` library, which works in the Edge runtime), checks the role, and redirects unauthorized users instantly. The real backend still re-checks every request; the edge guard is a fast first gate, not the only gate.

pairs.q: Why proxy API calls through /api instead of calling the backend directly?
pairs.a: Browsers enforce **SameSite cookie rules**: cookies are only sent to the same origin (domain+port) as the page. The frontend lives on one URL, the backend on another. By routing all API calls through `/api/*` on the frontend's own domain, the browser sees everything as 'same origin' and sends the login cookie. No CORS problems, no cookie rejected as 'third-party'.

pairs.q: How do health-trend charts know the 'safe range' for each test?
pairs.a: The backend stores reference ranges per test marker (e.g. HbA1c: 4–5.6%). When the patient's results API responds, it includes `referenceMin` and `referenceMax` for each marker. The Recharts component receives these values and draws a shaded `ReferenceArea` between them. Each data point is colored green (inside range), red (outside), or grey (unknown) by comparing its value against the same bounds.

pairs.q: What happens if the user denies location permission?
pairs.a: The `watchPosition` call immediately fires its error callback. The frontend catches this, sets `userLocation` to null, and falls back to sorting labs by **rating** (the next best default). No error message is shown — the sort selector just silently omits 'Nearest' as an active option. The app never breaks; it degrades gracefully.

title: Putting It All Together
subtitle: One website, three roles, built to be safe and clear
intro: The frontend is a clean, modern website that lets patients, labs, and admins each do their job, while keeping logins safe and the experience smooth.
bullets: Next.js + React + TypeScript give a fast, reliable, mistake-resistant website.
Login is protected by an unreadable cookie, an edge role-guard, and a same-origin proxy.
The AI assistant streams answers live and shows clickable lab and test cards.
Clever browser-side maths (haversine) finds the nearest labs instantly and privately.
Charts, toasts, skeletons, and friendly errors make it feel trustworthy for medical users.
